content.problemStatement:
Sereja has an undirected graph consisting of NNN vertices and MMM edges. Sereja is interested in one question: is it possible to delete edges from the graph in a way such that the degree of each vertex in the final graph is exactly 111?

The degree of any vertex in the graph is defined as the number of edges connected to that vertex.

Please help Sereja.
constraints: There can be multiple edges but no self-loops in the graph
content.inputFormat: The first line contains a single integer T, the number of test cases. Each test case contains two space-separated integers N and M, representing the number of vertices and edges in the graph, respectively. The next M lines contain two space-separated integers U and V, representing an edge connecting vertex U and vertex V.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, print YES if it is possible to delete edges from the graph in a way such that the degree of each vertex in the final graph is exactly 1, otherwise print NO.

sampleTestCases.explanation: For the first test case, we can delete one edge to make the degree of each vertex 1. For the second test case, it is not possible to delete edges to make the degree of each vertex 1. For the third test case, we can delete edges to make the degree of each vertex 1.
It is not possible to delete edges to make the degree of each vertex 1.
We can delete no edges to make the degree of each vertex 1.

solution.approach: To solve this problem, we can use the concept of perfect matching in graph theory. A perfect matching is a subset of the edges in a graph such that every vertex is incident to exactly one edge in the subset. We can check if a perfect matching exists in the graph by trying to match each vertex with another vertex. If we can match all vertices, then a perfect matching exists, and we can delete edges to make the degree of each vertex 1.
solution.timeComplexity: O(T * M * N) per test case, O(T * M * N) total. The time complexity is due to the nested loops used to check for perfect matching.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N) — the dominant structure is the adjacency list representation of the graph.
